item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the notification! I'm good with these changes. :3

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. We have a few crosslinks to propose here. The first one is turning the mention of Dr. Kiryu into a link to the heavily related Kiryu Labs Hub. Secondly, SCP-1457 features very heavily in the tales Waking and Slumbering, which can be in-line linked towards. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Dr. Kiryu and D-Class personnel D-19352 are seated within SCP-1457’s containment area. SCP-1457 rests on D-19352’s hand, wings unfolded.

To this:

Dr. Kiryu and D-Class personnel D-19352 are seated within SCP-1457’s containment area. SCP-1457 rests on D-19352’s hand, wings unfolded.

Changing this:

SCP-1457 is capable of transferring memories between humans through skin contact. Test results thus far invariably indicate that the memories transferred pertain to experiences involving isolation and tend to incite a desire for sympathy or company in the affected party.

To this:

SCP-1457 is capable of transferring memories between humans through skin contact. Test results thus far invariably indicate that the memories transferred pertain to experiences involving isolation and tend to incite a desire for sympathy or company in the affected party.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I found the butterfly on my porch one day. Probably got attacked by the birds in the neighborhood. I kept it in a little clear plastic drawer and gave it juice and pieces of ripe fruit to snack on. Sometimes I'd put it on my window screen to get some fresh air, but I had to stop doing it so often when birds would start flying up to the window and trying to peck out the butterfly.

I'm glad you liked the article!

That picture :(

I'm pretty sure butterflies can't survive from tattered wings, unfortunately. Unless they are, like, hand-fed in a terrarium. So you taking care of it is awesome.

Anyway, this is really great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww, poor butterfly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I award you +1 feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Butterflies actually have quite a bit of symbolism behind them—they were associated with souls by the Ancient Greeks, and they embody the concept of metamorphosis in meditation and visualization exercises. Plus some of them have pretty neat patterns and names. Yayyyyy!

Some of the people I've met on this site need to start writing for publishers, I tells ya.

Incidentally, some of the people here have gotten published and/or write professionally. I think sandrewswann is a professional author (though his member page just lists SCPs he's written…) and the Acquisitions series written by SRegan is practically a novella.

(now if wikidot's interface would notify me of replies, that would be nice :T)

Agreed. :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Using that kind of passion to drive your writing speaks for itself in the final product, I think.
yes, there are a couple butterfly skips, but everyone LIKES them, and reading them I've never thought "ugh another butterfly".

Some of the people I've met on this site need to start writing for publishers, I tells ya.

(now if wikidot's interface would notify me of replies, that would be nice :T)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I might have answered this question somewhere in my staff askthread, but the gist of it is I have lots of fond memories of butterflies.

When I was in elementary school, I caught little skipper butterflies with my friends in the soccer fields. One time I rescued a purple butterfly under the lunch tables from boys who were pelting it with snack food (I later wrote a story about the experience and it was my only A+ story in all of fifth grade). In high school, Mourning Cloaks began to show up in my neighborhood, and I started raising the late-stage caterpillars through their pupa phase. One of my college application essays was written about a butterfly with a broken wing I found on my porch and took care of for several months (that's the butterfly in 1457's picture!)

Reminiscing aside, I'm glade you like this article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm noticing a pattern with all of the skips I've seen you write, Zyn.
I'm loving the copious use of Lepidoptera, but (being a long time lurker-turned-member) I gotta ask:

What's with you and butterflies, Lady?

(Oh, and +1, considering the little flutter-butter is laying down some top tier life lessons to an unwilling [or unWITTING] researcher)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still though: it just doesn't really stick to me :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, but see, it doesn't actually make them feel sad, it incites memories that make them feel lonely. If people feel sad, it's on them, not the butterfly shaking them going "SAD FEEL SAD!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno…something doesn't really stick to me about it except being a butterfly that transfers memories and makes people sad. The tone, to me, seems a bit funky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This broke my heart a little. I have never upvoted something so fast in my life.

item.title: Regarding Image
item.content:encoded: For the record, the image used belongs to me. The butterfly pictured is named Sol. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you.

I thought that towards the end of the journal the 'dialogue' was a little 'on the nose' in terms of the doctor & the butterfly just stating exactly what they were thinking,

To be honest, I wasn't really sure about those sections, but I was trying to portray the duality (i.e. the purpose of "sharing" being enlightenment versus simply throwing one's own woes upon another) of the butterfly in the sense that even though it seems rather straightforward, the doctor still doesn't perceive the intentions of the butterfly completely correctly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite excellent. Also, nice touch with using a 'broken' butterfly for this.

I thought that towards the end of the journal the 'dialogue' was a little 'on the nose' in terms of the doctor & the butterfly just stating exactly what they were thinking, but the rest of the journal works so well that I didn't care. I really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm, I guess these were the ones that I remembered most. :D

And oops, awkward

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, thank you.

There's definitely a significant number of SCPs out there that convey emotion. Dmatix's SCP-1440 and Soulless's SCP-1252 are two of my favorites.

also… I'm female. Just so you know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Why is this not a +100 already, it's deserving of it.

I just made the +100 vote on 348 (after finding it via the "Leaks" thread) and you, sir, are an amazing author. One of the only ones on the site who can make an SCP that conveys so much emotion (the other notable one being the homesick alien).

You're awesome, can't wait to see what else you write. I would +1 YOU if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another philosophical post!

It takes someone who really, truly cares. And sometimes, that's all it takes.

Very much so. My inspiration behind this SCP was basically the need to have someone there to listen to whatever woes one has, regardless of whether that person can do anything to help or not.

The lesson, the next time the Doctor finds someone cold and alone, someone who needs a hand, help them.

Somewhat.

Potential spoilers
close

The main misunderstanding the doctor has is that the butterfly projects memories only because it needs to feed. Granted, it does sustain its unusually long lifespan through feeding off empathy, but my personal take on it is that the butterfly wants to make humans more aware of the results of their actions and the effects on those around them (hence the first memory the butterfly ever retained, involving the destruction of the forest).

Also, props to you for the poetry quote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read the article. Meh. I read addendum 1. Meh. I read the journal.

I upvoted as hard as I could.

The doctor is missing another important part of the point: Not only is pain lessened by sharing it, but it's also a lesson, one we must learn. The lesson, the next time the Doctor finds someone cold and alone, someone who needs a hand, help them. Make them feel better; pain is lessened also by preventing it. Truly it is a generous man, who gives naught but himself to lessen the pain of others, for he gives himself not in parts, but as a whole. It takes someone who really, truly cares. And sometimes, that's all it takes.

To quote a wise man,
"I always lose my memories but I never lose my way, and half the time I'm blind but I can see the light of day.
I never look too far ahead for what's already there, maybe all I need to set me free is tell you that I care.
I used to wander in the night to see what I could find, but by the time the sunrise came the night had made me blind.
It wasn't till I hit the top that I had hit my bottom; you cannot set your heartaches free till first of all you've got em."

Confusing philosophical post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For what it's worth, I found this beautiful. And yet, still, scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. Nothing constructive to add, I just love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'twas feexed. Soul is junior feexer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I'm glad to hear that; when I first posted the article, I was unsure of whether the journal was overdone (or, as stated earlier, "too cheesy").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could upvote twice, btw. It's not often someone posts an SCP that evokes the sort of emotions this one did for me without overdoing it. It was just right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tbh, the journal is what made it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Regarding the list coincidence… Whoa. I didn't notice when I picked out the number for this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read 1470, and knowing that the author of that article likes this one is, well, awesome. Thanks, Sorts!

I think the ultimate idea here is that suffering is lessened by sharing it with others. The butterfly is not feeding, it's sharing. But if you take on too much, you'll be crippled by it like the researcher was.

I had the idea that the butterfly didn't want just anyone to share its stories with; it wanted someone who would care enough to keep experiencing all the memories, until the butterfly "trusted" the person enough to share the very first memories of loneliness it ever experienced.

Of course, it didn't really work out for the doctor because he was only interacting with the butterfly for research purposes, and the years and years of memories did overwhelm him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: YES.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's a difference. Also I like this a lot, and I like it pretty much purely for the journal.

The telepathic spider was about a spider getting wigged out when it was talking to the equivalent of eldritch beings from beyond the veil (and said beings actually sympathizing with the spider on account of also dealing with things that are equally beyond their own ability to understand).

This is also about a researcher trying to understand what the butterfly is transmitting… but it seems like the researcher is overwhelmed by the content rather than understanding it.

I think the ultimate idea here is that suffering is lessened by sharing it with others. The butterfly is not feeding, it's sharing. But if you take on too much, you'll be crippled by it like the researcher was.

edit: also it's kinda neat that the telepathic bugs are so close together in the list completely by coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your choice to make 1457 more sympathetic was a wise move, methinks. I do think the journal is a tad too long, as you said, but it gets the point across. 1655, along with my second SCP, will probably get a rewrite sometime in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Precisely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I came across 1655 while reading through articles to check if my idea had been done before. I definitely agree that 1655 is more malicious, which is why I decided to give 1457 a backstory that would make its actions somewhat pitiable.

This is probably a bit better written, I must say.

Wow, thank you! (That means a lot to me, seeing as I really liked the way you structured 1655. I personally think that the journal excerpt for 1457 is too long, but I can't for the life of me think of how to trim it down further…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly!

(though the butterfly itself would like some sympathy too. It's had its share of sad memories.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a lot of my own first article on the site: SCP-1655. 1655 is far more malicious, and its effects are much more potent, but the idea at the core is similar- the fear and sorrow of loneliness. This is probably a bit better written, I must say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Psychic butterfly that feeds you sorrowful images to make you feel bad and then eats your empathy.

I can dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Best, butterfly, ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Journals are useful in that you can in fact break some tone in order to express what is necessary to express. In journals, you can make them sound like actual people rather than mere reports. This also applies to Interviews. I think it would be better to have a concern in which does the scientist sound like a person? Does he sound like a scientist taking notes in a journal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see. However, I still don't think the article would be able to stand on its own without the journal, simply because a lot of the significance of the SCP itself is contained in the journal entries.

Anything you could suggest in regards to making the journal sound more clinical?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think SCPs are supposed to be clinical and cold, and if it is a research journal in the addendum, then that should be clinical too. The personalization of the journal seems better for a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like the doctor didn't realise that he'd reached the end. That second-to-last scene must have been horrifying from a betterfly's perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt that the addendum gave more information about the SCP in a way that didn't make the delivery blatantly obvious (i.e. The butterfly feeds on human emotions. The butterfly can communicate. The butterfly can transmit memories of its own; humans are not the only beings that experience tragedy.)

If you feel that the addenda detract from the clinical tone so much that it merits a downvote, could you offer some advice on how to incorporate the addenda into the article itself? (Unless, of course, you don't think the interview or journal should be included at all. Personally, I'm inclined to keep the addenda, but if enough people comment that I should do otherwise, I can always create a separate Tale for the addenda and simply link the article to it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted and generally like, but the entire time all I could think of was this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I echo the comments that this would be a much better piece without the addendum and the journals, they really detract from the clinical tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepiest…hug…ever

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feex'd?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here you go!
Click to hide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I need a hug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That definitely makes sense, although I'm unsure of how well the article and journal would be able to stand alone as a separate article and tale.

I agree with you on the journal being too long; I'll trim those parts down as soon as I'm able. Thanks for the specific feedback on this—I was having a lot of trouble trying to decide which parts weren't needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you would have a better piece by losing all of the journal. Maybe split that off into a Tale? The prose is a bit overwrought ("cried and laughed and shared our dreams," "paperwork streaming onto the floor," "I won’t be able to stand it if their smiles disappear"), whch comes off as cheesy, and also the journal starts up a whole new story at about the point when the regular SCP part should be ending, then goes on for a long time, which tries the patience a bit. Plus, I think that either 1) it's a bad idea to try to resolve the back story as thoroughly as you have here, or 2) the idea's okay but it wasn't executed well.

That said, this is only my opinion, and other people may disagree, so don't do anything drastic just yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1470. I'm not sure how similar these two are. The spider is truly telepathic, while this butterfly is empathic, and seems to get nutrition from its abilities.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid I'm not familiar with that one. Link please? (though I can try looking for it myself if it's a bother to you).

In the meantime, any advice on what I could change to make this SCP different from the spider?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, Too much like the telepathic spider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know, just how vivid is a memory for the average person? Speaking personally, most of my memories are sort of vague, and the ones I remember better are more abstract not-pictures centering around smells and such.

I sort of wanted the article to read in a way that would imply that the memories are vivid to the point where recalling them would trigger emotions (but the writer of the journal misinterprets the SCP's effect and believes the SCP itself embeds the emotions in)

I think you'd do best to making a note, something sciency (sorry, I can't help with tone) explaining how the person has total… recall of the events that took place.

I was thinking of that. What made me hesitant was thinking if a short note stating the subject has total recall would be more effective than seeing the different scenarios the SCP has "collected".

Read it over again, and if the Journal entries do not sway you in any particular way, delete them

Like I mentioned before, I tried to provide a variety of memories to show the extent of the different scenarios the SCP encountered. As such, if the memories are excessive, out of the ones currently in the journal, which do you think should be trimmed out?
